is [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") music made in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). It is, along with its counterparts elsewhere in the world, part of what has been called the "[third wave of ska](/wiki/Third_wave_ska "Third wave ska") [that] combines the traditional [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") [Club](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub") sound with [metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_music "Punk music"), [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk_music "Funk music"), and/or [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music")."[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Japanese ska** is [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") music made in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). It is, along with its counterparts elsewhere in the world, part of what has been called the "[third wave of ska](/wiki/Third_wave_ska "Third wave ska") [that] combines the traditional [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") [Club](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub") sound with [metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_music "Punk music"), [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), [funk](/wiki/Funk_music "Funk music"), and/or [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music")."[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_ska&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Artists")]

Having been formed in the 1980s and enjoying international success (including extensive touring in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe")), the group [Tokyo Ska Paradise Orchestra](/wiki/Tokyo_Ska_Paradise_Orchestra "Tokyo Ska Paradise Orchestra") is arguably the original and best-known Japanese ska ensemble.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[175R](/wiki/175R "175R"), [Beat Crusaders](/wiki/Beat_Crusaders "Beat Crusaders"), [The Boom](/wiki/The_Boom "The Boom"), [Kemuri](/wiki/Kemuri "Kemuri"), [Kojima](/wiki/Haruna_Kojima "Haruna Kojima"), [Lisa](/wiki/LISA_(Japanese_musician,_born_1974) "LISA (Japanese musician, born 1974)"), [Mongol800](/wiki/Mongol800 "Mongol800"), [Tsu Shi Ma Mi Re](/wiki/Tsu_Shi_Ma_Mi_Re "Tsu Shi Ma Mi Re"), [Shaka Labbits](/w/index.php?title=Shaka_Labbits&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shaka Labbits (page does not exist)"), [Gelugugu](/w/index.php?title=Gelugugu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gelugugu (page does not exist)"), [Potshot](/wiki/Potshot_(band) "Potshot (band)"), [Yum!Yum!Orange](/w/index.php?title=Yum!Yum!Orange&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yum!Yum!Orange (page does not exist)"), [Yukihiro Takahashi](/wiki/Yukihiro_Takahashi "Yukihiro Takahashi"), [Ska Ska Club](/w/index.php?title=Ska_Ska_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ska Ska Club (page does not exist)"), Kasutera and [Oreskaband](/wiki/Oreskaband "Oreskaband") are other Japanese artists that have performed ska (though not all exclusively).

Japan has had a proliferation of album-making ska bands. Besides those mentioned elsewhere in this article, one source lists the [Clippers](/w/index.php?title=Clippers_(band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clippers (band) (page does not exist)"), [Coke Head Hipsters](/w/index.php?title=Coke_Head_Hipsters&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coke Head Hipsters (page does not exist)"), [the Drops](/w/index.php?title=The_Drops&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Drops (page does not exist)"), [Duck Missile](/w/index.php?title=Duck_Missile&action=edit&redlink=1 "Duck Missile (page does not exist)"), [Fruity](/w/index.php?title=Fruity_(band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fruity (band) (page does not exist)"), [Gelugugu](/w/index.php?title=Gelugugu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gelugugu (page does not exist)"), [La Ppisch](/w/index.php?title=La_Ppisch&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Ppisch (page does not exist)"), [Life Bal](/w/index.php?title=Life_Bal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Life Bal (page does not exist)"), [Mustang AKA](/w/index.php?title=Mustang_AKA&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mustang AKA (page does not exist)"), [Nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine_(band) "Nicotine (band)"), [Oi-Skall Mates](/w/index.php?title=Oi-Skall_Mates&action=edit&redlink=1 "Oi-Skall Mates (page does not exist)"), [Piala](/w/index.php?title=Piala_(band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Piala (band) (page does not exist)"), [Rude Bones](/w/index.php?title=Rude_Bones&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rude Bones (page does not exist)"), [Shoulder Hopper](/w/index.php?title=Shoulder_Hopper&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shoulder Hopper (page does not exist)"), [Scafull King](/w/index.php?title=Scafull_King&action=edit&redlink=1 "Scafull King (page does not exist)"), [Skatroits](/w/index.php?title=Skatroits&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skatroits (page does not exist)"), [Sprocket Wheel](/w/index.php?title=Sprocket_Wheel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sprocket Wheel (page does not exist)"), Dallax, [The Rollings](/w/index.php?title=The_Rollings&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Rollings (page does not exist)"), [Step by Step](/w/index.php?title=Step_by_Step_(band)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Step by Step (band) (page does not exist)"), [Sk@ymate's](/w/index.php?title=Sk@ymate%27s&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sk@ymate's (page does not exist)"), [Skapontas](/w/index.php?title=Skapontas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skapontas (page does not exist)"), [Tropical Gorilla](/w/index.php?title=Tropical_Gorilla&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tropical Gorilla (page does not exist)"), [midnightPumpkin](/wiki/MidnightPumpkin "MidnightPumpkin"), [SPYZZ](/w/index.php?title=SPYZZ&action=edit&redlink=1 "SPYZZ (page does not exist)"), and [Young Punch](/wiki/Young_Punch "Young Punch").[[5]](#cite_note-page-5)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_ska&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

According to one [music critic](/wiki/Music_critic "Music critic"), Japanese ska "music translates well, as they have fully embraced the [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") [horned](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument")-based ska [idiom](/wiki/Idiom "Idiom")".[[6]](#cite_note-6) Another remarks "from traditional ska to [ska-punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk"), Japan has a thriving scene that is ready to invade America.[[7]](#cite_note-stein-7)

A source at [MIT](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") describes:

> Although far less popular than [J-pop](/wiki/J-pop "J-pop"), J-rock ([Japanese rock](/wiki/Japanese_rock "Japanese rock")), or [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), ska music has a definite presence in Japan. Emerging about a decade ago with forerunner bands [Ska-Flames](/w/index.php?title=Ska-Flames&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ska-Flames (page does not exist)") and Tokyo Ska Paradise Orchestra, the Japanese ska scene saw a huge popular surge in 1997 (mirroring the burst of popularity in the United States) which quickly died out. However, many Japanese ska bands continue to persist despite a lack of mainstream support. [The Determinations](/w/index.php?title=The_Determinations&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Determinations (page does not exist)"), [The Side Burns](/w/index.php?title=The_Side_Burns&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Side Burns (page does not exist)"), and [Blue Beat Players](/w/index.php?title=Blue_Beat_Players&action=edit&redlink=1 "Blue Beat Players (page does not exist)") are Japanese ska bands popular among fans of the genre.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In Japan, both Japanese and traditional Jamaican ska enjoy some popularity. An article in *[Jamaica Observer](/wiki/Jamaica_Observer "Jamaica Observer")* quoted a Japanese music seller as saying:

> Ska is very big in Japan and we have about five Japanese ska bands in my city. We just had [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster") performing there [...] Japanese people love Ska [...] in fact, they love every period of music from Jamaica.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

A Japanese pop authority remarked that some see:

> ...similarities between the highly stylized "[skanking](/wiki/Skank_(dance) "Skank (dance)")" dance style associated with ska music and Japanese "[bon odori](/wiki/Bon_Festival "Bon Festival")" [festival dances](/wiki/Japanese_festivals "Japanese festivals"). (Ska music and ska-punk have long been favorites in Japan, and today there are countless Japanese ska and ska-punk bands.) And [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae")’s [syncopated rhythms](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation") are echoed in the rhythm of [Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture") music -- so much so that Okinawa music is sometimes called "Japanese reggae."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Although the Japanese ska scene is centered in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") there are ska venues elsewhere in Japan, even in smaller cities such as [Kisarazu](/wiki/Kisarazu "Kisarazu") in [Chiba Prefecture](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_ska&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classification")]

While some sources (such as one of the quotes above) discern Japanese ska from [Japanese pop](/wiki/Japanese_pop "Japanese pop"), other sources lump them together. While this classification is contrary to the fact that ska and [punk music](/wiki/Punk_music "Punk music") are rarely, if ever, classified as [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") in most of the world, it is congruous with the practice of considering Japanese pop to encompass other [genres](/wiki/Genre "Genre") which are often regarded as separate from pop music in the United States and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), among them rock, [urban](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") and [electronic dance music](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music").

The consideration of Japanese ska as a subgenre of Japanese pop may be due, at least in part, to bands typically considered ska releasing songs with a definite [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") sound. Sometimes, this genre-spanning occurs within a single song. For example, [Snail Ramp](/w/index.php?title=Snail_Ramp&action=edit&redlink=1 "Snail Ramp (page does not exist)") (a "typical ska punk" band from [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")) has a song called "Hotaru no Hikari" featuring [Air](/wiki/Air_(Japanese_band) "Air (Japanese band)"). For the most part, this is a ska punk version of "[Auld Lang Syne](/wiki/Auld_Lang_Syne "Auld Lang Syne")" with Japanese lyrics. Sections of the song, however, divert from this musical form and instead have a sound akin to [boy band](/wiki/Boy_band "Boy band") music.[[7]](#cite_note-stein-7)[[nb 1]](#cite_note-12)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14)

According to the filing scheme used at [Tower Records](/wiki/Tower_Records "Tower Records") in Tokyo, all Japanese ska is classified as [hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk"), even that with the laid-back sound that typifies early Jamaican ska music from the early [1960s](/wiki/1960s_in_music "1960s in music").[[13]](#cite_note-15)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_ska&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** This sort of stylistic departure, when it occurs in American and European punk music, may be regarded as [ironic](/wiki/Irony "Irony") and may even be so in this case.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** ["While the reference above calls Snail Ramp a "typical ska punk" band, a review of one of their albums from a different source remarks "A couple of the songs had ska-nature, and turned out pretty well for stuff from a non-ska band.""](http://lahosken.san-francisco.ca.us/frivolity/reviews/jamu/snail_ramp.html).
